summary: I work at Shenandoah National Park as the Interpretive Programs Manager, where I handle guided walks, talks, and campfire programs. During the September 11 attacks in 2001, I was also part of the Type 2 Incident Management Team and was called away from Shenandoah to assist in Philadelphia. Chester Mikus, who also works here at Shenandoah, accompanied me on that mission. The park was part of the Northeast Region that we supported during the incident.

I served as the Public Information Officer on a Type 2 Incident Management Team, which is assembled by the National Park Service to manage significant incidents affecting parks. Around a year and a half before September 11, 2001, the Northeast and Eastern Coast regions combined to form these all-risk teams. Our team included Rick Brown as incident commander and others like Carl Merchant and Chester Mikus. During 9/11, we were activated to coordinate resources and information primarily in the Northeast and Southeast Regions.

The National Park Service organized the Type 2 Incident Management Teams to handle emergencies and significant events in parks. Around 2000, they merged several regional teams on the East Coast to improve response capabilities. During the September 11 attacks, the NPS coordinated closely with regional offices and the Type 1 teams to manage resources and security at affected sites like Federal Hall and the Liberty Bell. I was part of this effort as a team member and Public Information Officer.

I was part of the Type 2 Incident Management Team representing the Northeast Region of the National Park Service during the September 11 attacks. This region was combined with the Eastern Coast to form a larger all-risk management team about a year and a half before 9/11. Our team coordinated with parks in this region, assessing risks and security needs, especially for sites like the Liberty Bell and Federal Hall. We also communicated with the Type 1 team to provide updates and resource information.

The Type 2 Incident Management Team I was on covers the Eastern United States, which includes the Northeast and Southeast Regions. This consolidation happened about a year and a half before the September 11 attacks to better manage incidents across a broader area. During 9/11, our team worked to coordinate resources and security assessments across this vast region, ensuring parks were prepared and supported. We also collaborated with other teams and regional offices within this area.

Our Type 2 Incident Management Team operates based on the Incident Command System, which structures how we manage significant incidents. This system assigns roles like incident commander, operations chief, planning chief, and safety officer to ensure clear responsibilities. During the September 11 attacks, this system allowed us to coordinate effectively with other teams and agencies, maintaining order and communication. I served as the Public Information Officer within this structure.

As part of the Type 2 Incident Management Team, we underwent a 40-hour training course at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center, which prepared us for managing incidents. This training was essential for understanding our roles within the Incident Command System and for effective team coordination. The FLETC training helped us be ready for events like the September 11 attacks, where we had to respond quickly and efficiently.

Rick Brown was the incident commander of our Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks. He led the team that included myself as Public Information Officer and others like Carl Merchant and Chester Mikus. Rick was responsible for making decisions with the superintendent's approval and coordinating our efforts in the Northeast and Southeast Regions. His leadership was crucial in managing the complex response during that time.

Carl Merchant served as the planning section chief on our Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks. He was responsible for organizing the planning efforts and helping to coordinate resources and information across the parks in our region. Working alongside Rick Brown and others, Carl played a key role in ensuring our team operated smoothly during the crisis.

Chester Mikus works with me at Shenandoah National Park and was part of our Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks. He accompanied me on the trip to Philadelphia to help manage the incident response. Chester was one of the section chiefs on the team, and his local knowledge and experience were invaluable during the deployment.

Ben Morgan was our safety officer on the Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks. He was actually from Everglades National Park but happened to be in Manhattan when the attacks occurred. He contacted us to offer his assistance and was able to provide on-site safety oversight during the incident response in New York City.

Ben Morgan, who was our safety officer during the September 11 response, came from Everglades National Park. Although the Everglades is far from the Northeast, Ben was in Manhattan during the attacks and was able to join our team on site. This shows how resources and personnel from parks like Everglades can be mobilized to assist in incidents anywhere in the country.

Manhattan was the central location affected during the September 11 attacks, where the Twin Towers were located. Our Type 2 Incident Management Team supported sites in Manhattan, including Federal Hall, which was very close to the towers. Ben Morgan was actually in Manhattan during the attacks and served as our safety officer on site. Communication and resource coordination in Manhattan were challenging due to disrupted phone services.

Will Reynolds was the operations section chief on our Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks. He was responsible for overseeing the operational aspects of our response, coordinating resources and personnel. His role was critical in managing the day-to-day activities as we supported affected parks and sites in the Northeast Region.

The September 11 attacks in 2001 were a pivotal event that activated our Type 2 Incident Management Team. I was called on that day after hearing the news on the radio and was deployed to Philadelphia with Chester Mikus to assist in managing the incident. Our team coordinated resources, assessed risks at various national park sites like the Liberty Bell and Federal Hall, and communicated with the Type 1 team to support the national response. It was a challenging time with disrupted communications and uncertainty about further attacks.

Philadelphia was one of the key locations where our Type 2 Incident Management Team was deployed during the September 11 attacks. Chester Mikus and I drove there on the evening of September 11 to begin our work. We coordinated with regional offices and helped manage resources and security for national park sites in the area. Philadelphia was a critical hub for our response efforts in the Northeast Region.

Dale Ditmanson was the associate regional director who gave us our delegation of authority during the September 11 response. The regional director, Marie Rust, was in Florida and unable to return, so Dale stepped in to authorize our Type 2 Incident Management Team to manage the incident. His role was crucial in empowering us to act on behalf of the National Park Service in the Northeast Region.

Marie Rust was the regional director for the Northeast Region of the National Park Service during the September 11 attacks. She was in Florida at the time and could not return to the region, so Dale Ditmanson, the associate regional director, acted in her stead. Although she was not directly involved on site, her leadership role was important in the overall regional response.

The Liberty Bell was one of the national park sites we monitored during the September 11 attacks. Our team assessed its security and risk level compared to other sites like Shenandoah or Federal Hall. We coordinated resources to ensure the Liberty Bell was protected, given the uncertainty about further terrorist threats. It was part of our broader responsibility to safeguard important landmarks in the Northeast Region.

Federal Hall National Memorial, located near the Twin Towers in Manhattan, was one of the sites heavily impacted by the September 11 attacks. Our team helped coordinate cleaning, security, and resource needs for Federal Hall. Because it was so close to the attack site, it required immediate attention and support from the National Park Service. We ordered resources to help it get back to normal operations as quickly as possible.

The Twin Towers in Manhattan were the focal point of the September 11 attacks and the epicenter of the incident we responded to. Federal Hall, which is very close to the towers, needed significant support in the aftermath. Our Type 2 Incident Management Team coordinated resources and security around this area. The attacks on the Twin Towers created a massive disruption that affected national park sites and required extensive coordination.

The National Capital Region had its own Type 2 Incident Management Team during the September 11 attacks, separate from our Northeast and Southeast Regions. While we coordinated resources and information across the Northeast and Southeast, the National Capital Region managed its own response. However, we did swap resources when needed, especially to support law enforcement like the U.S. Park Police.

During the September 11 attacks, the U.S. Park Police needed additional security support, and our Type 2 Incident Management Team helped provide resources. Although the National Capital Region had its own team, we shared personnel and equipment as needed across regions. The U.S. Park Police were a critical law enforcement presence at affected sites, and supporting them was part of our mission.
